figures.caption: Figure 1: User Interface of Privy. Privy serves as a side panel of the webpage, which consists of three main components: (A) Action labels extracted from the privacy policy surface available rights as selectable options; (B) After selecting a right, Privy provides step-by-step guidance to the relevant service (here, Privy provides a direct link to Google Takeout based on the Google’s privacy policy); (C) Users can request on-demand policy context, including legal references, verbatim policy excerpts, and rights education.
Figure 4: Result of SUS quesionaire. For the negative statements (first fve), lower ratings are better. For the positive statements (bottom fve), higher is better
Table 1: Comparison of Privy with prior work across four capabilities identified in our review. = fully supported, G# = partially supported, # = not supported.

Figure 2: Design Framework of Privy. (a) Privy automatically locates and analyzes a website’s privacy policy, extracting applicable rights as action labels; (b) When a user selects a right, Privy adapts its guidance to the organization’s stated mechanism: providing direct links, drafting emails, or highlighting UI elements for step-by-step navigation; (c) At any point, users can request policy context including a plain-language legal reference, the verbatim policy excerpt from the target website, and right education notes about the right being exercised.

Figure 3: The three strategies that Privy will provide to the users for exercising their rights: (a) Link: Privy presents a direct URL to the organization’s privacy portal and explains what to expect upon visiting; (b) Email: Privy generates a ready-to-use email template with pre-filled recipient, subject, and body. (c) Navigation: Privy highlights relevant UI elements on the page and provides step-by-step instructions across page transitions.
